segments.s203-g241: بلشکرگه خویش بازآمدند is structured as prepositional/place phrase + possessive + verb: بلشکرگه (army-camp) + خویش‌ش (their own) and the plural past verb بازآمدند 'they returned'. In Persian the possessor is attached as a clitic (ش) to خوی, yielding 'their own', and بازآمدند is 3rd-person plural simple past meaning 'came back'.
segments.s203-g242: Here two coordinated noun phrases precede the plural verb آمدند: گرازنده 'boar-hunters' (agent noun) و 'and' بزم ساز 'makers/performers of the feast' (a compound meaning musicians/entertainers), then آمدند 'they came'. The compound بزم ساز literally 'feast-maker' functions as a label for entertainers; the verb is again 3rd-person plural simple past.
segments.s203-g243: ز is the poetic preposition 'from', followed by the genitive phrase گردان ایران 'the troop/ranks of Iran'. برآمد is a 3rd-person singular past verb meaning 'arose/issued forth', and خروش is the noun 'cry/roar' that is the thing which arose. Word order here (preposition + source phrase + verb + noun) reflects a poetic inversion typical in classical Persian.
segments.s203-g244: همی is an emphatic/archaic particle (here giving vividness/emphasis), کر is the adjective 'deaf', and شد is the copular/auxiliary 'became' (past). از introduces the cause 'because of/from' and links to ناله کوس 'the drum's lament' with گوش 'ear' as the affected organ. The whole phrase means 'the ear became deaf because of the drum's lament.'
segments.s203-g113: بخوردند is a past plural verbal form (from خوردن) used idiomatically here to mean 'they took/accepted' (classical usage with سوگند). سوگندهای is the plural ezafe-less noun 'oaths/vows' and گران is an adjective modifying it meaning 'weighty/solemn'. Word order is verb + object phrase (verb first, then the noun phrase it governs).
segments.s203-g114: که introduces a subordinate content clause 'that ...'. پیمان شکستن is an infinitival/nominal phrase 'the breaking of the covenant' (پیمان = covenant, شکستن = the act of breaking). نبود is past negated existence ('there was not' / 'would not be'), and اندران ('among them' / 'in that') localizes the negation to the group — together: 'that breaking the covenant was not among them.'
segments.s203-g115: کزین is a compound prepositional phrase 'from this' (کز + این). رزمگه is the locative noun 'battlefield'. برنتابیم is a negative potential/ability form in 1st person plural 'we cannot bear/ endure' (بر- + نتابیم). روی here functions as 'face/turning (our face)' — literally 'we will not turn our face from this battlefield.'
segments.s203-g116: گر begins a conditional clause 'if'. از introduces source 'from', گرز خون اندر آید is the protasis 'blood comes from the mace' (گرز = mace; خون = blood; اندر آید = comes in/comes forth). بجوی is an imperative/jussive root meaning 'seek' or 'look for' and follows the conditional as the apodosis (command/result clause). The clause thus reads 'if blood comes from the mace, seek (it).'
segments.s203-g117: وزان is a conjunctive/locative 'and/from that'. جایگه 'place/position' followed by ران 'flank; thigh' names the part targeted. بیفشاردند is the past plural 'they pressed/pushed' (بی-فشاردند = they applied pressure / forced in). Grammatically this is a simple past clause: from that place they pressed the flanks.
segments.s203-g118: برزم here is an older/poetic locative/verb form relating to the battle (classical usage), followed by اندرون 'inside' and گرز 'maces'. بگذاردند is past plural 'they placed/put'. Together the clause states they 'placed maces within the interior of the combat.' Note the verb comes at the end (typical Persian word order: object/locative before the past verb).
segments.s203-g119: ز هر سو = 'from every side' (ز = from, هر سو = every direction/side). سپه بیکران is a noun phrase 'hosts/armies countless' (سپه = army/host, بیکران = boundless/countless). کشته شد is a passive/perfect construction 'were slain' (کشته = killed; شد = became/was). The clause describes mass slaughter from all directions.
segments.s203-g120: زمانه refers to 'the times' or 'fortune'. همی is an older adverbial particle often marking continuous or emphatic aspect. بر بدی گشته شد literally 'turned toward/became bad' (بر = toward, بدی = evil/badness, گشته شد = turned / had become). Together: 'the times had turned to evil/ill fortune.' Note the compound verbal participle گشته plus شد expresses the change/result.
segments.s203-g169: سپاه اندر آمد is a simple subject + verb phrase: سپاه (the host/army) + اندر آمد (entered/came in), with اندر here marking 'in/into' (poetic). بگرد درفش follows as a participial/verb phrase meaning 'turning/around the banner' — بگرد is a verbal form meaning 'turn/around' (poetic/intransitive usage) and درفش is the object/goal 'the standard/banner'. The sequence reads as one continuous narrative action: the army came in, turning about the banner.
segments.s203-g170: هوا شد is a copular construction: هوا (the air/atmosphere) + شد (became), i.e. 'a (certain) atmosphere arose.' ز گرد سواران is a causal/locative phrase 'from the dust/cloud of the horsemen' — ز = 'from' and گرد here means 'dust' (poetic). بنفش modifies the resulting atmosphere: 'purple' or 'violet', so the whole clause says an atmosphere/tint grew purple from the riders' dust.
segments.s203-g171: دگر باره is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning 'once more/again' (دگر = again, باره = time/occasion). از جای برخاستند is a verb phrase: از جای ('from their place/position') + برخاستند (they rose/rose up/left) — برخاستند is past tense, third-person plural, indicating collective movement.
segments.s203-g172: بران is a locative/relational adverb 'upon that / thereon' linking to the preceding scene; دشت رزمی نو آراستند is the main action: دشت رزمی نو ('a new martial/plain' — adjective order: دشت (plain) + رزمی (martial) + نو (new)) and آراستند (they arrayed/arranged/adorned) is the past plural verb meaning they made ready or drew up a new battle formation on that plain.
segments.s203-g173: به پیش سپه is a locative/relational phrase 'before/in front of the army' (به + پیش + سپه). کشته شد is a passive past construction: کشته (killed/slain) + شد (auxiliary 'was'), and ریونیز is the personal name of the one killed; the word order is typical Persian passive (object/participant before شد).
segments.s203-g174: که begins a relative/explanatory clause 'which/that'; کاوس را uses the object/experiencer marker را to mark Kay Kavus as the one affected. بد here means 'bad/ill/unfortunate' (i.e. grievous) and چو ('as/like') introduces the simile comparing the loss to 'جان عزیز' ('a dear/beloved life'). Grammatically this clause explains the effect of the killing on Kay Kavus.
segments.s203-g175: یکی is an indefinite introducer 'one / a certain.' تاجور here functions as a proper name/title (not the common noun 'merchant' in this context) and is followed by a genitival description: شاه کهتر پسر = 'the king's younger son' (شاه = king, کهتر = lesser/younger, پسر = son). Word order is nominal apposition: name followed by identifying phrase.
segments.s203-g176: This short segment gives the individual's personal lineage/affection: نیاز (here a proper name: Niyaz) + فریبرز (Fariburz, the father's name) implies 'Niyaz, [son of] Fariburz'; و links the following phrase جان پدر ('the father's life' i.e. 'the delight/heart of his father'), a poetic way of saying he was dearly loved by his father. The cluster is nominal/appositional rather than a finite clause.
segments.s203-g217: 'چو' here is the conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. 'سیصد تن' is a numeral + measure word construction meaning 'three hundred persons'; 'تن' functions as 'people/person'. 'از تخم افراسیاب' is a genitive phrase 'from the seed/of the stock of Afrasiab' (تخم used metaphorically = descendants). Word order is numeral + unit + 'از' + source.
segments.s203-g218: 'کجا' is a rhetorical interrogative here ('where/whither' = 'where had ... gone?'). 'بختشان' is the possessed noun 'their fortune/fate' serving as the theme. 'اندر آمد' is a classical intransitive verbal phrase 'came upon/befell' (literally 'came into'). 'بخواب' is used as a noun/nominal state 'sleep' (literally 'into sleep' — i.e. 'fell asleep' or 'was slain/did badly' in the idiomatic sense of fortune being asleep). The clause literally asks where their fate went, answering that it went 'to sleep'.
segments.s203-g219: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. 'خویشان پیران' is a genitive phrase 'the relatives/kinsmen of Piran' (پیران here names or identifies the group). 'چو' again means 'when' or 'as', and 'نهصد سوار' is 'nine hundred horsemen' (numeral + noun). The whole phrase sets up the group and their nominal strength.
segments.s203-g220: The verb phrase 'کم آمد' literally 'came few' (i.e. 'few came' or 'they were few/insufficient'). 'برین روز' is an adverbial phrase 'on that day' (بر = on/upon, ین = poetic variant 'that'). 'در کارزار' further specifies location 'in the battlefield'. Word order places the predicate ('کم آمد') before the adverbials for emphasis, typical in poetry.
segments.s203-g221: 'همان' ('that/that very') modifies 'دست' (here 'hand' used idiomatically for 'power, influence'). 'پیران' is in the genitive ('of Piran'); 'بد' is the predicate adjective 'bad/ill-fated'. The conjunction 'و' links to the following clause; 'روز اوی' = 'his day' (اوی = possessive 'his'). The phrase says Piran's power was ill-starred and so was his day.
segments.s203-g222: 'ازان' = 'from that/of that' connects the following description to what was just said. 'اختر' is 'star' and 'گیتی‌افروز' is a compound adjective 'world-illuminating' (an epithet describing the star). The final 'اوی' again is the possessive 'his', so the clause literally says 'his day was of that world-illuminating star' — i.e. his fate/day was determined by that bright star.
segments.s203-g223: 'نبد' is an archaic negative/existential verb 'was not / there was not' placed before the nominal phrase. 'روز پیکار ایرانیان' is the noun phrase 'a day of combat for the Iranians' (روز = day, پیکار = battle/struggle, ایرانیان = the Iranians). Together this means 'there was no day of battle for the Iranians' (i.e. no victorious/normal day of fighting remained for them).
segments.s203-g224: 'ازان' = 'from that/thereupon' links back to the prior events. 'جنگ جستن' uses جنگ (war) plus the infinitival/agentive جستن ('to seek/wage') meaning 'waging that war' or 'that war's activity'. 'سرآمد زمان' is a fixed poetic phrase 'the culmination/end of time/age' (سرآمد as 'conclusion/culmination' + زمان 'time/era'). The clause says that out of that war the end (or turning point) of an age came about.
segments.s203-g121: This is a reporting clause in the order typical of classical Persian: indirect object marked by به ('to') comes first, then the person addressed (بیژن), then the adverbial چنين ('thus'), and finally گفت ('said'). گودرز پیر is the speaker's name plus the epithet 'the elder' (پیر) placed after گفت, a common poetic order (lit. 'to Bijan thus said Goudarz, the elder').
segments.s203-g122: This is the quoted imperative clause introduced by کز, here functioning as the contraction of 'که از' = 'from/that from'; ایدر 'here' gives the departure point, برو is a 2nd‑person imperative 'go', زود is an adverb 'quickly', and برسان is another imperative 'bring/deliver'; تیر is the direct object 'arrow'. The verbs are imperative commands addressed to Bijan.
segments.s203-g123: بسوی + proper name gives direction 'toward Fریبرز'. برکش عنان is an imperative clause: برکش is an imperative of برکشیدن 'pull/take up', and عنان 'reins' is the object — together 'pull in the reins' or 'turn (the horse) toward...'. The verb is elliptically addressing the rider to steer toward Fereburz.
segments.s203-g124: بپیش من آر is another sequence of imperatives/requests: بپیش 'before/present to', من 'me', and آر here functions as an imperative 'bring/put' (classical usage), so 'bring before me'. اختر کاویان is an honorific epithet meaning 'Kayanian star' (a title), the object being requested to be presented. Word order places the directional phrase before the object and title.
segments.s203-g125: مگر here introduces an exception/conditional 'unless'. خود 'self' emphasizes that it is Fereburz personally. با آن درفش 'with that banner' uses با as the instrumental 'with' and آن درفش 'that banner' as the accompaniment. The phrase sets up the conditional agent and means.
segments.s203-g126: بیاید is an optative/subjunctive sense 'may/should come' (here: 'should come'); کند is the verb 'do/make' that links to the following object, and روی دشمن بنفش is a compound where روی 'face' takes the genitive دشمن 'of the enemy' and بنفش 'purple' is a predicate/adjective — literally 'make the enemy's face purple' (i.e. strike or put them to shame). The clause completes the conditional sense begun by مگر.
segments.s203-g127: چو بشنید is a temporal clause 'when he heard' (چو = 'when'); بشنید is past tense 'heard'. The subject name بیژن follows, and برانگیخت اسپ is the verb + object: برانگیخت 'roused/roused up' (here of a mount) and اسپ 'horse/steed' — so 'Bijan, when he heard, roused/spurred his horse.'
segments.s203-g128: This is a brief nominative statement: بیامد 'came' is the verb, followed by the epithet بکردار 'virtuous/ of good conduct' modifying the proper name آذرگشسپ; the phrase names the arriving person — 'Azar‑Gushasp the virtuous came.' Word order places the verb before the epithet + name in this poetic line.
segments.s203-g177: Compound theme 'سر و تاج او' (head and crown of him) appears before the verb; 'اندر آمد' is a past-tense verbal phrase literally 'came into' used here idiomatically as 'fell'. The final prepositional compound 'بخاک' = 'to the ground/onto the earth' completes the verbal idea.
segments.s203-g178: 'بسی' is an adverbial intensifier modifying the noun phrase 'نامور جامه' ('famed garments'). The verb phrase is a light verb construction 'کردند چاک' (literally 'they made [them] torn') — 'چاک کردن' = 'to rend/tear'. Word order is OBJ–VERB (poetic inversion): object(s) then the verb.
segments.s203-g179: 'ازان پس' is a temporal adverbial meaning 'after that' or 'thereupon'. 'خروشی برآورد' is a fixed poetic phrase meaning 'uttered/raised a cry' where 'برآورد' is past tense; the proper name 'گیو' follows the verb (poetic inversion) and is the subject/agent.
segments.s203-g180: The speaker begins a vocative with 'که‌ای' (an archaic/poetic form used to address people). 'نامداران و گردان نیو' is the vocative phrase: 'renowned ones and the troop/band of ney (reed‑flutes)', with 'نیو' here the musical instrument 'ney'.
segments.s203-g181: 'چنویی نبود' = 'there was not such' (چنویی = 'such/like this'); 'نبود' is past negative 'was not'. 'اندرین رزمگاه' is a locative prepositional phrase 'in this battlefield' (اندرین = 'in this', رزمگاه = 'battlefield').
segments.s203-g182: A nominal description in apposition: 'جوان و سرافراز و فرزند شاه' lists attributes in coordinate order ('young and proud and son of the king'), describing the person who is asserted not to be present. Adjectives and nouns are simply coordinated with 'و'.
segments.s203-g183: 'نبیره جهاندار کاوس پیر' is a nominal chain: 'نبیره' (scion/offspring) qualified by 'جهاندار' (the sovereign/conqueror) and the name 'کاوس' followed by the epithet 'پیر' ('the old'). Word order is determiner/epithet after the head noun in typical epic style.
segments.s203-g184: The clause 'سه تن کشته شد' is a passive construction: 'سه تن' (three persons) + 'کشته شد' (were killed), where 'شد' functions as auxiliary forming the passive. The following short phrase 'زار بر خیره خیر' links reactions: 'زار' can be read as 'the wretched/they who wailed' or as 'wailed' (verb), 'بر خیره' = 'upon/toward the staring/astounded [onlookers]', and 'خیر' here is an interjectional/lexical particle expressing evaluative sense ('goodness' / an exclamatory marker); the exact nuance is poetic and succinct.
segments.s203-g233: بدو گفت = 'he said to him': بدو is the dative/oblique pronoun 'to him' placed before the verb; گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (classical narrative past). هین is an interjection meaning 'halt/wait', and برنشین is an imperative 'sit (down) beside/above'. از پسم literally 'from after me' = 'by my side / next to me'.
segments.s203-g234: گرامی‌تر از تو نباشد کسم is a comparative clause: گرامی‌تر is the comparative 'more dear/honored', از تو functions as 'than you', نباشد here expresses existence-negation ('there is not / there is no one'), and کسم literally 'my person' = 'my equal/anyone to me'. Together: 'There is no one dearer to me than you.'
segments.s203-g235: نشستند هر دو بران بارگی: نشستند is the past plural 'they sat'; هر دو 'both/either both of them'; بران is a locative/old prepositional form 'on/upon that', and بارگی is 'leaf/foliage' — 'the two sat on that leaf.'
segments.s203-g236: چو خورشید شد تیره یکبارگی: چو here is temporal 'when'; خورشید 'the sun'; شد 'became' (past of شدن); تیره 'dark'; یکبارگی 'suddenly, all at once' — 'when the sun suddenly grew dark.'
segments.s203-g237: همه سوی آن دامن کوهسار: همه 'everyone/all', سوی 'toward', آن دامن کوهسار 'that skirt/foothill of the mountain' — 'all toward that mountain's slopes.'
segments.s203-g238: گریزان برفتند برگشته کار: گریزان is an adverbial/participial adjective 'fleeing' or 'in flight', برفتند is the past plural 'they went off/they fled', and برگشته کار literally 'the turned-back affair' — i.e. the situation was reversed/ended. The noun کار is qualified by برگشته ('turned back'), producing a summary statement about the outcome.
segments.s203-g239: سواران ترکان همه شاددل: سواران ترکان 'the horsemen of the Turks' (genitive relation), همه 'all', شاددل an adjective 'cheerful' — 'the Turkish horsemen, all cheerful.'
segments.s203-g240: ز رنج و ز غم گشته آزاددل: ز = 'from' (poetic), رنج و غم 'suffering and sorrow', گشته 'became/has become' (resultative), and آزاددل 'free‑hearted/light‑hearted' describing the resulting state. Literally: 'from suffering and sorrow become free‑hearted.'
segments.s203-g209: Poetic word order places the preposition ز ('from/of') before the group: 'ز گودرزیان هشت تن زنده بود' = 'of the Gudarzis eight persons were alive.' تن is the human classifier 'persons'; زنده is a predicate adjective 'alive'; بود is the past-tense copula agreeing with the (implied) plural subject.
segments.s203-g210: بران = بر + آن (poetic contraction) ‘on/thereon/at that’; رزمگه is ‘battlefield’; دیگر here means ‘other/elsewhere’; افگنده is a past participle ‘cast/laid/scattered’ and بود is the auxiliary giving a past meaning: 'had been cast' / 'were laid' (past passive/resultative).
segments.s203-g211: هم 'also' begins the clause; از تخمه گیو = 'from the seed/offspring of Giv' (تخمه used as 'offspring/lineage'); چون before a numeral is a poetic/colloquial marker meaning 'about' or 'approximately' here; the sequence noun + numeral is normal in verse.
segments.s203-g212: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding numeral ('who/which were'); بودند is the plural past 'they were'; زیبای is an adjective 'beautiful/handsome'; دیهیم is an adjective 'rural, of the villages' (i.e. countrymen); و گنج links the last attribute 'and treasure/wealth' (i.e. they were wealthy).
segments.s203-g213: Parallel to the previous clause: هم 'also'; از تخم کاوس 'from the seed/lineage of Kavus'; هفتاد مرد places the numeral after the noun ('seventy men'). مرد here carries the heroic/warrior sense.
segments.s203-g214: سواران 'horsemen' and شیران literally 'lions' used metaphorically for valorous fighters; روز نبرد is a genitival phrase 'day of battle' (i.e. in battle), so the whole string characterizes those seventy as battle-hardened cavalry/warriors.
segments.s203-g215: جز 'except' + از 'from/of' introduces an exception. ریونیز is a rare/uncertain word or name here (the text uses an uncommon epithet/place), followed by آن 'that' and سر تاجدار 'crowned head' (سر used as 'head/person' or leader). The whole phrase names the single exceptional person.
segments.s203-g216: سزدگر is used impersonally/agentively meaning 'it is fitting/one who deems fit'; نیاید here is the negative subjunctive/jussive 'should not come' (i.e. 'should not be added'); کسی in negative contexts functions as 'anyone/no one' (here: 'no one'); در شمار = 'into the count/tally.' Together: 'it is not fitting that anyone be included in the count.'
segments.s203-g137: یکی بانگ برزد به بیژن که رو — This is a short clause: یکی (someone) is the agent, بانگ برزد is a past action 'uttered a cry' with برزد an archaic verb meaning 'to make/sound' (here 'raised/uttered'), and به بیژن marks the addressee 'to Bijan.' The following که + رو begins the quoted imperative; رو here is the archaic imperative 'رَو' (go/turn), elliptical and typical in epic diction.
segments.s203-g138: که در کار تندی و در جنگ نو — که functions as 'for/because' introducing the reason for the shouted command. در کار is an idiom 'in (one's) deeds/behavior' and تندی here means 'haste/rashness' (character flaw). بعد از و introduces the second parallel adverbial 'in war' (در جنگ) and نو = 'new' i.e. 'inexperienced' when applied to a fighter.
segments.s203-g139: مرا شاه داد این درفش و سپاه — Word order is the familiar Persian SOV variant where مرا (me, accusative) is fronted for emphasis, شاه is the subject, داد is the past tense 'gave,' and این درفش و سپاه is the direct object phrase 'this banner and (an) army.' Note that مرا is one token here split in the provided list (see senses).
segments.s203-g140: همین پهلوانی و تخت و کلاه — همین 'this very' emphasizes what follows; پهلوانی here means 'heroic prowess' (not literal wrestling), and تخت و کلاه list additional possessions 'throne and helmet' (typical formulaic enumeration). The conjunction و simply strings the items together.
segments.s203-g141: درفش از در بیژن گیو نیست — درفش 'the standard' is the grammatical topic. از در بیژن is a prepositional phrase 'from/at Bijan's door/house' (in epic Persian در can mean 'gate/house'). گیو appears as a proper name placed before the verb; with نیست the clause says 'Giv is not' or 'it is not Giv,' producing an ambiguous order common in older Persian where names can be topicalized. The overall sense is negative existence/possession (نیست).
segments.s203-g142: نه اندر جهان سربسر نیو نیست — نه negates the clause; اندر جهان سربسر is an emphatic adverbial 'throughout the world' (اندر = in, سربسر = everywhere/every part), نیو here is the noun 'ney' (reed‑flute), and نیست again marks nonexistence 'does not exist.' In context this is a general negation layered onto the previous clause.
segments.s203-g143: یکی تیغ بگرفت بیژن بنفش — یکی 'someone' is the agent, تیغ 'a sword' is the object, and بگرفت is the past 'seized/took up.' The phrase بیژن بنفش is an appositional tag 'Bijan, (the) purple‑clad' — the adjective بنفش follows the name in epic style and qualifies him.
segments.s203-g144: بزد ناگهان بر میان درفش — بزد is the past of زدن 'he struck/hit'; ناگهان 'suddenly' is an adverb; بر میان درفش is a prepositional phrase 'at/on the middle of the standard' with میان 'middle' governed by بر. The clause describes a sudden blow aimed at the center of the banner.
segments.s203-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' (here marking a temporal subordinate clause). آمد is the 3rd-person singular past of آمدن 'to come/arrive'. سر ماه is a compound noun 'head of the month' (a poetic way to mark the time/turn of the month), and هنگام جنگ is a time phrase 'at the time of battle' that specifies the occasion. The whole segment is a temporal clause setting the scene for what follows.
segments.s203-g2: ز here is the preposition 'from/away from'. پیمان is the noun 'covenant/oath'. بگشتند is the 3rd-person plural past of برگشتن with the prefix ب- (poetic/autonomous past) meaning 'they turned (away)' or 'they went back'; it functions as the main verb of this clause. و links the next phrase; از نام و ننگ repeats the preposition 'from' and lists 'name (reputation/fame) and ننگ (shame)', indicating they abandoned the covenant and disregarded honour/reproach. In short, this clause states the action that followed the temporal clause.
segments.s203-g3: خروشی is a noun meaning 'a roar/clamour' (the -ی here forms an abstract noun). برآمد is the 3rd-person singular past 'arose/issued forth'. ز هر دو سپاه is a source phrase 'from both hosts/armies' (ز = from; هر دو = both; سپاه = host/army). The structure is verb-final-poetic: noun + verb + source phrase.
segments.s203-g4: برفتند is the 3rd-person plural past of رفتن with the verbal prefix بر- (they departed/advanced). یکسر is an adverb modifying the verb meaning 'entirely/at once' (intensifier). سوی is the preposition 'toward', and رزمگاه is the object 'battlefield/place of contest'. Word order places the verb before its adverbial modifier and direction phrase in this poetic line.
segments.s203-g5: ز بس is a poetic preposition + quantifier phrase meaning 'from so much/so many' (indicating cause or abundance). ناله names the cry/crying; بوق is 'trumpet/horn blast'; و links items; هندی درای is a coordinated phrase referring to an Indian drum/instrument — here a compound of 'هندی' (Indian) and 'درای' (an instrument word in the poem). The whole noun phrase lists the loud noises that cause the effect in the next segment.
segments.s203-g6: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('indeed/so/just') placed before the subject for emphasis. آسمان is the subject 'the sky/heaven'. اندر آمد is a compound verbal phrase (اندر + آمدن) meaning 'moved/was displaced / came down from' and is in past tense; ز جای is the source phrase 'from its place'. Together they describe an extreme effect (the heavens themselves moved from their place).
segments.s203-g7: هم is the additive particle 'also'. از is the preposition 'from'. یال اسپان is a coordinated noun phrase 'the manes of the horses' (یال = mane; اسپان = horses; the ezāfe is omitted in poetic style). و دست و عنان continues the list 'and (from) hand and reins'. The segment is a series of 'from' phrases enumerating sources of noise and action; the nouns are coordinated by و.
segments.s203-g8: ز again marks 'from' and introduces a further list: گوپال و تیغ و کمان و سنان (a string of nouns joined by و). Grammatically this is simple coordination following the preposition; each noun is another source/instrument in the scene. In poetry the repeated ز/از emphasizes the multiplicity of sources.
segments.s203-g49: ببارید is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural/formal verb used metaphorically here ('it rained' → 'it rained down'); the verb is fronted for poetic effect. تیر is the direct theme ('arrows'). از کمان سران is an instrumental/genitive phrase 'from the bows of the chiefs' (possessor following the possessed: کمانِ سران), with از marking source.
segments.s203-g50: بران is an archaic/poetic contraction meaning 'بر آن' ('upon them' / 'upon that'); it introduces the target of the raining arrows. نامداران جوشن‌وران is a noun phrase in which نامداران (renowned ones) qualifies جوشن‌وران (armored warriors) — i.e. 'renowned, mail-clad warriors.' Word order is noun modifier + head.
segments.s203-g51: چنان شد که is an idiomatic subordinating phrase 'so/thus it came to pass that...' (شد = 'happened'/'became'). کس here functions negatively with the following negated verb (کس ... ندید) and means 'no one.' روی کشور is the object phrase 'the face/appearance of the country.' Negation is expressed in the past form ندید ('did not see').
segments.s203-g52: ز بس (poetic) introduces the cause/degree 'because of so many / from the abundance of.' کشتگان is the subject 'the slain' and شد is the past 'became' (resultative). زمین ناپدید means 'the ground/earth became vanished' — the verb is placed before the subject for poetic rhythm (verb earlier than the more neutral زمین ناپدید شد).
segments.s203-g53: یکی is an indefinite subject 'one / someone.' پشت بر دیگری is a prepositional/relational phrase 'back against the other' with بر='against.' برنگاشت is a past verb here meaning 'placed/turned' (an archaic/poetic past form meaning he leaned/put), completing the clause: 'one put his back against another.' Word order here is subject + object phrase + verb (classical/persuasive word order).
segments.s203-g54: نه negates the following past verb بگذاشت; together they mean 'nor did he leave/abandon.' آن جایگه را is the definite object 'that place/position' with را marking it as object. که داشت is a relative clause 'which he had/held' (داشت = past 'had'). The whole segment says he did not abandon the post he held.
segments.s203-g55: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus'; گفت is the past verb 'said'). هومان is the subject (the speaker) and به فرشیدورد marks the indirect object/recipient with به ('to Farshidvard'). Word order places the verb before the subject and indirect object for poetic emphasis.
segments.s203-g56: که introduces the content clause ('that ...'). با is the instrumental 'with/by'; قلبگه is a poetic noun 'the heart's seat / the heart' (the target). جست is a verbal root meaning 'to leap/ to spring' used here in a clause governed by the modal باید ('must'); باید expresses necessity and is followed by نبرد, here functioning as the verb 'to fight' (classical nominal/verb form). The clause therefore means 'that one must fight by leaping at the heart.' Note the poetic inversion of verb elements (جست ... باید نبرد) compared with more prosaic modern order.
segments.s203-g161: بگفتند is a 3rd-person plural past-tense verb meaning 'they said' (here used to introduce a question/utterance). The two با before the names functions colloquially/poetically to mark the addressees — 'to/with Giv and with Gostaham' — so the phrase is effectively 'they said to Giv and to Gostaham.' The coordinating و simply links the two names.
segments.s203-g162: سواران is the plural noun 'horsemen/riders' (the thing being asked about). که introduces a relative or interrogative clause 'who/which', and بود ند is the past copula 'were' (3rd plural). با او بهم is a prepositional phrase: با او = 'with him' and بهم (here) means 'together' — so the clause asks which riders 'were together with him'. Word order places the question-clause after the addressees introduced in the previous segment.
segments.s203-g163: که opens a subordinate (explanatory/declared) clause 'that'. مان is a poetic noun meaning 'the remainder/remnant' (those left). رفت is the simple past 'went' (here part of the predicate describing the remnant's movement), and باید is the modal 'must/ought (to)'. بتوران is the contracted prepositional phrase 'به توران' = 'to Turan', and سپاه (here postposed) names the force involved ('army/host'). Together they express: 'that the remnant must go to Turan (with the army)'.
segments.s203-g164: ربودن is the infinitive/masdar 'to snatch away' or 'to seize'. از ایشان is the prepositional phrase 'from them' specifying the object of seizure. همی is a frequent classical/poetic adverb meaning 'continually / indeed / in this way' and adds a continuous or emphatic sense. تاج و گاه are the objects 'crown (تاج) and seat/place (گاه)' — a fixed pair meaning rulership/sovereignty; تاج is literal 'crown' and گاه here functions as the 'seat' or 'place' of rule. The infinitive phrase states the intended action: 'to snatch from them the crown and seat.'
segments.s203-g165: ز is a poetic preposition meaning 'from'. گردان ایران is a genitival phrase 'the troop of Iran' (گردان = troop/unit). دلاور modifies سران and here follows it in a compact poetic word order: 'دلاور سران' = 'valiant chiefs/leaders'. The whole segment therefore reads 'from Iran's troop (came) the valiant chiefs'.
segments.s203-g166: برفتند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they went/advanced'. بسیار is modifying نیزه‌وران and means 'many' (numerous). نیزه‌وران is a plural agent noun 'spearmen' (fighters armed with lances). The word order (verb then quantity + noun) is a compact poetic sequence: 'they advanced — many spearmen.'
segments.s203-g167: بکشتند is a 3rd-person plural past 'they killed'. ز ایشان means 'of/from them' (indicating the victims). فراوان is an adjective/adverb 'many/plentiful' qualifying سوار ('horsemen/riders'), so the phrase says they killed 'many horsemen of them.' The verb-front order (verb then object phrase) is common in epic/poetic Persian.
segments.s203-g168: بیامد is a 3rd-person singular past 'came' (verb before subject is normal in narrative). ز ره is the source phrase 'from the road/way'. بیژن is the proper name Bijan, and نامدار is a postpositive adjective 'renowned/famous'. Together: 'From the road came Bijan the renowned.' (Contextually a number of mounted men may be implied as well.)
segments.s203-g145: بدو here is the old/prepositional pronoun 'به + او' (to/at it), referring back to the thing struck (the standard); نیمه کرد is a past tense compound where کرد (to make/do) follows نیمه (half) to mean 'made into a half' = 'cut in half'. اختر کاویان is the agent placed after the verb in poetic word order: 'the Kayanian Akhtar (did this)'.
segments.s203-g146: یکی نیمه برداشت is literal past 'he took up/removed one half' (یکی = one, برداشت = lifting/removal). گرد از میان functions as an adverbial phrase 'away/from the middle' (گرد here indicating motion/around/away and از میان 'from the middle'), describing where the half was taken.
segments.s203-g147: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. The particle که here introduces purpose ('so that' / 'in order that'), so که آرد benazd سپاه means 'that he might bring it near the army' — آرد is classical present/optative meaning 'brings' (used with که for purpose), بنزد = 'to the presence of / near', سپاه = 'the army/host'.
segments.s203-g148: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. ترکان (the Turks) is the subject; بدیدند is past 'they saw'. اختر براه is a locative phrase 'Akhtar on the road / Akhtar en route' (براه = 'on the road, en route'), so the clause means 'when the Turks saw Akhtar on the road'.
segments.s203-g149: یکی at the start is the indefinite numeral 'one/a certain'. شیردل is a compound adjective 'lion‑hearted' (brave). لشکری جنگجوی stacks two nouns/adjectives describing the man: لشکری 'of the host/army' (military) and جنگجوی 'warrior/fighter' — together 'a lion‑hearted warrior of the army'.
segments.s203-g150: همه سوی ... نهادند روی is an idiomatic past construction 'they all set/turned their faces toward ...': همه 'all/everyone', سوی 'toward', بیژن is the object (Bijan), نهادند is past plural 'they placed/set' used here with روی 'face' to mean 'they turned (their) faces'. The verb follows the object in this poetic order.
segments.s203-g151: کشیدند is past plural 'they drew' — in the martial context it commonly means 'drew (weapons) / drew into action'. Here it takes two objects joined by و: گوپال (a personal name, Gopāl) and تیغ بنفش 'the purple sword', meaning they brought Gopāl and the purple sword into the fight.
segments.s203-g152: به پیکار indicates purpose/direction 'to (the) battle' or 'into combat'. آن کاویانی درفش is a noun phrase 'that Kayanian banner/standard' (آن = that/the), so the whole phrase 'به پیکار آن کاویانی درفش' means 'to fight that Kayanian banner' or 'against that Kayanian standard'.
segments.s203-g17: This is a nominative/agent phrase: یلان (yelān) 'the warriors/heroic men' is the head, followed by a with‑phrase introduced by با + a personal name plus title (فریبرز کاوس شاه). The sequence 'فریبرز کاوس شاه' places the proper name before the royal title (typical of epic word order) and با marks accompaniment ('with Fereyberz, King Kavus').
segments.s203-g18: This is a prepositional/location phrase with poetic doubling: درفش 'the standard' is located 'از پس پشت' — a classical compound of prepositions meaning 'from behind / out of the rear' (از + پس + پشت), followed by در قلبگاه 'in the heart/core (of the formation)'. There is no finite verb here; it's a verbless appositional/location clause describing where the banner is.
segments.s203-g19: Simple main clause: proper name فریبرز is subject, با لشکر خویش 'with his army' is an accompanying prepositional phrase (خوی + enclitic ش = خویش 'his own'). گفت is the past tense verb 'said' introducing reported speech. (Note: the printed sense list for گفت did not include the verbal sense 'said'.)
segments.s203-g20: Reported content clause introduced by که 'that'. The clause uses an older word order: ما را (dative/experiencer marked by را) + هنرها (the skills/arts) + شد (became) + اندر نهفت (inside/hidden). بردنِ 'ما را' here signals the experiencer ('to us' / 'for us'), so the sense is 'that our skills became hidden to/within us.' شد is the past intransitive 'became' and نهفت is an adjectival/predicative past participle 'hidden'.
segments.s203-g21: A temporal/emphatic opening: یک امروز 'one day / this very day' (a poetic way to mean 'today/this day'), followed by a simile introduced with چون 'like' + شیر 'lion' modifying the following predicate. جنگ آوریم is 1pl present/subjunctive/formal 'we bring/wage war' (آوریم = 'we bring'/'we cause'), so the phrase reads 'this day, like a lion of battle we wage war.'
segments.s203-g22: Parallel main clause with inverted order: جهان 'the world' is the semantic object affected, بر + بداندیش 'against the ill‑minded' (بر marking 'against/upon'), and تنگ آوریم is a causative/resultative phrase 'we make narrow/press' (literally 'bring tight'), i.e. 'we will put the malicious in straits' or 'we will oppress the ill‑minded.' The verb آوریم repeats as the 1pl predicate.
segments.s203-g23: Prepositional/time phrase: کزین 'from this' + ننگ 'disgrace/shame' gives the source; تا جاودان 'until eternal (time)' uses تا in its temporal 'until' sense, and بر سپاه 'upon the army' places the result/scene. The phrase sets the temporal and target frame for what follows.
segments.s203-g24: Personification + predicate: بخندد همی is an intransitive future/habitual form 'will laugh / will be laughing continually' (همی marks continual/ongoing action). Here بخندد is best read as 'laugh at' (to mock), and its agents are گرز و رومی کلاه 'the mace and the Roman helmet' — objects of scorn are personified as laughing at the army. Word order places the verb before the compound subject for poetic effect.
segments.s203-g33: This clause contains a simple past statement: تو (you) is the (second‑person) subject and گفتی is the past tense verb 'you said'. The remainder is a nominal clause functioning as the content of that saying: زمین (earth) + روی (face) + زنگی (dark / 'blackened') describe the state, and شدست is an archaic/heavy poetic form of 'شده است' (has become), placed after the predicate as is normal in classical Persian.
segments.s203-g34: This is a second coordinated predication introduced as a parallel to the previous clause: ستاره دل ('the star of the heart' — a metaphorical/epithetic noun) is the subject, پیل جنگی is a compound noun meaning 'war‑elephant' (پیل modified by جنگی), and شدست again is the archaic perfective 'has become'. Word order is Subject — Predicate (nominal) with the copular/auxiliary after the predicate.
segments.s203-g35: ز بس here is a prefatory locative/causal phrase: ز (from/of) + بس (an abundance / 'so many') followed by a coordination of weapons (نیزه و گرز و شمشیر) with the adjective تیز (sharp) placed after شمشیر to qualify it. Classical Persian often lists coordinated nouns then qualifies the last with an adjective.
segments.s203-g36: برآمد is a simple past/preterite verb meaning 'arose/issued forth'. همی is a classical enclitic/adverb here giving emphasis or continuousness ('indeed/still'), and از جهان رستخیز is the source/scene: از (from) + جهان (the world) + رستخیز (uprising/resurrection). Word order is verb then adverb then prepositional phrase marking origin.
segments.s203-g37: This clause begins with a prepositional phrase of origin: ز قلب سپه ('from the heart/core of the army'), followed by the proper name گیو as subject. شد is the past auxiliary/verb meaning 'came/became/moved' and پیش صف means 'to the front of the ranks' (پیش = before/in front of; صف = battle line). Classical Persian often places the source phrase before the action.
segments.s203-g38: This is a participial/coordinate description of manner: خروشان (roaring) is a verbal adjective describing how he advanced; و (and) links the next action; بر لب برآورده کف is a compressed classical phrasing meaning 'with the palm raised to (or brought to) the lip' — بر (on/to) + لب (lip) with برآورده ('brought up' / 'raised') modifying کف (palm). Note برآورده is used poetically here as a participle.
segments.s203-g39: This nominal phrase is compact: ابا is a verbal adjective/noun meaning 'reluctant / refusal' used predicatively or attributively, followed by the epithet نامداران (renowned ones) and the tribal/family name گودرزیان (the Gudarzis). In classical syntax an adjective/verb can precede the noun it qualifies for emphasis.
segments.s203-g40: کزیشان is a compound relative/prepositional phrase (کز = 'from/of which' + ایشان = 'them') meaning 'from them'. بدی راه is a noun phrase 'the bad/evil way' (بدی = evil/harm, راه = way/path), and سود و زیان = 'gain and loss' is coordinated. Grammatically this states result: 'from them came the bad way of profit and loss.'
segments.s203-g153: چنین گفت هومان is literally 'thus said Homan' (چنین = 'thus/so' before the verb). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' and Homan is the nominative name; the relative/subordinating که begins the following clause ('that/which'). Note the word order: adverb + verb + subject + relative marker, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s203-g154: This is a relative clause introduced by که ('that/which'). نیرویِ ایران is a genitive phrase 'the strength/power of Iran' (نیروی + ایران), بدو اندر است places that power 'in him' (بدو = 'to/within him', اندر = 'in/within') and است is the present/predicative 'is'. The clause literally: 'that the strength of Iran is within him.'
segments.s203-g155: درفش بنفش 'the purple banner' is a noun phrase (درفش + adjectival بنفش). ار is the older/poetic conditional 'if'. بچنگ آوریم is a compound: بچَنگ 'into the grasp' + آوریم 'we bring/bring about' — together 'if we bring (it) into our grasp' or 'if we seize (it)'. The verb is present-subjunctive/optative 'آوریم' used for the protasis of the conditional.
segments.s203-g156: جهان جمله 'the whole world' (جهان + جمله = 'all the world'), بر شاه تنگ آوریم is the apodosis: بر شاه تنگ = 'tight upon the king' and آوریم (same verbal form) 'we bring' meaning 'we make' or 'we cause to be.' So the clause means 'we will make the whole world press/tighten upon the king.' Note the causative/resultive sense of آوریم here.
segments.s203-g157: کمان را uses the object marker را after کمان 'the bow' (direct object). بزه کرد is a poetic/past action: here it functions like 'drew/struck (the) bow' — بزه is an archaic/poetic form (not the ordinary noun 'crime') and کرد is the past auxiliary 'did/made' forming the past action. چو گرد 'چو' = 'when/as' and گرد = 'around/about/turn' — 'when he turned about.'
segments.s203-g158: بریشان is a contracted form of بر ایشان ('upon them') functioning as the prepositional object. یکی تیرباران = 'a single/one shower of arrows' (یکی = 'one/a single', تیرباران = 'shower of arrows'), and بکرد is the past verb 'he made/did' meaning here 'he sent' or 'he let fly' that volley. Word order places the object-phrase before the verb, typical in Persian.
segments.s203-g159: سپه 'the army' is the subject. یکسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely/at once'. از تیر او دور شد is a verbal phrase 'moved away from his arrow(s)' (از = 'from', تیر او = 'his arrows', دور شد = 'became distant/was removed'). The past شد marks the completed change of state.
segments.s203-g160: همی is an emphatic/temporal particle (here 'at that/then/indeed'), followed by گرگ درنده را where گرگ درنده = 'the ferocious wolf' and را marks it as the (affected) object/topic. سور شد literally 'became a feast' (سور = 'feast/banquet', شد = 'became') — a poetic way of saying the wolf was made into a feast or there was a feast for (on) the wolf.
segments.s203-g201: برآویخت is a past-tense (perfective) verb meaning 'sprang up/rose' (a poetic past). چون introduces a simile ('like'), followed by شیر ('lion') as the standard of comparison. بهرام is a proper name placed after the simile; گرد here functions as an epithet-forming/qualifying element attached to بهرام (poetic usage) rather than the literal adverb 'around'. The whole chunk reads: 'he sprang up like (the) lion Bahram(-gird)'.
segments.s203-g202: بنیزه is an instrumental noun meaning 'with a spear' (instrument placed before the clause). بریشان is a contracted prepositional phrase (بر + ایشان) meaning 'against them' (target/dative). یکی modifies حمله as 'a single/one attack'. برد here is the past of بردن in an archaic/poetic sense 'carried out / made' (verb placed at the end); the sequence is Instrument — Target — Numeral + Object — Verb.
segments.s203-g203: بنوک سنان is a noun phrase 'the tip/point (بنوک) of the spear (سنان)' (noun + noun in genitive relation). تاج را uses the postposed object marker را to mark 'the crown' as definite. برگرفت is a past-tense verb 'took up / seized' placed after its object, so: 'the spear-point seized the crown.'
segments.s203-g204: دو لشکر is the subject 'two armies'. بدو is a dative/adverbial 'to him / toward him'. مانده is a participial/resultant adjective 'remained / were left' (state resulting from action). اندر شگفت is a locative/adverbial phrase 'in wonder / astonishment' modifying مانده. Word order: Subject — (direction) — predicate — adverbial.
segments.s203-g205: همی بود is an archaic/epic continuous past construction meaning 'it was so / it continued so' (همی adds durative/emphatic sense). زان گونه is a locution 'in that manner / thus'. تا is the subordinating 'until' introducing the temporal clause تیره گشت, where گشت is the past of گشتن meaning 'became' and تیره 'dark', so: 'it continued so until it became dark.'
segments.s203-g206: همی again gives an emphatic/durative flavor. دیده is the subject 'the eye / the eyes'. از تیرگی is a prepositional phrase 'from the darkness' marking cause or source. خیره گشت is an intransitive past construction 'became fixed / astonished / dazed' (خیره as predicative adjective + گشت 'became'), so: 'the eyes became dazed/astonished from the darkness.'
segments.s203-g207: چنین هر زمانی is a temporal adverbial 'thus / whenever' (چنین 'so', هر زمانی 'whenever / at every time'). برآشوفتند is a plural past verb (بر-آشوفتند) 'they rushed upon' or 'they flew at' — a poetic compound verb where بر- intensifies the motion toward the object. The clause means 'whenever this happened, they rushed forth.'
segments.s203-g208: همی is emphatic/durative. بر سر is a prepositional phrase 'upon the head(s) / at the head(s)'. یکدگر is the reciprocal 'one another'. کوفتند is the past plural verb 'they struck / they smote' (object follows the prepositional phrase); together: 'they kept striking one another on the head.'
segments.s203-g57: فریبرز (proper name) is the sentence subject; باید is the modal auxiliarly 'must/ought' that governs the following action. کزان is a poetic prepositional compound ('from which' / 'from that'), and قلبگاه is a compound noun 'heart / core'; the whole phrase sets the origin/place for the action that follows (i.e. 'must, from that core...'). Word order is somewhat topicalized: subject + modal + place-origin phrase.
segments.s203-g58: گریزان is a participial adjective used adverbially meaning 'fleeing / slipping away'; بیاید is the present/subjunctive form 'come' used after باید to express the required action ('must come' / 'must slip in'). ز (poetic form of از) marks origin 'from', پشت سپاه = 'the rear/behind of the army' — a prepositional phrase attached to the verb. Together: 'must, slipping away, come from the rear of the host.'
segments.s203-g59: پس is a discourse linker 'then/therefore'. آسان بود is the past-tense predicate 'was easy' (آسان = adjective, بود = past of 'to be'). جنگ is the noun 'battle/fight' and با میمنه ('with/on the right side') is a prepositional phrase specifying the flank; the order is slightly topicalized (predicate before the noun) typical of epic/poetic style.
segments.s203-g60: بچنگ آید is a verbal compound meaning 'come into the grasp / fall into (someone's) hands' (بـ + چنگ = 'into grasp' + آید 'comes/will come'). آن رزمگاه و بنه is the direct object phrase 'that battlefield and (its) camp/cluster' linked by و; آن is a demonstrative 'that'. The clause states the result: those places will be taken/come into their hands.
segments.s203-g61: برفتند is the simple past, 3rd‑person plural 'they went / advanced'. پس is a connective 'then'. تا here marks the limit 'until' and is followed by بقلب سپاه: the prefix بـ (بـِ) + قلب 'into the heart / core' + سپاه 'of the army' — a motion/directional phrase indicating how far they advanced.
segments.s203-g62: بجنگ is به + جنگ and indicates purpose/direction 'to battle / for battle'. The following names are the opponent: فریبرز is the personal name (object of the fighting), and کاوس شاه is an appositional title 'Kavus, the king' (name followed by the title شاه). The construction expresses purpose: 'in order to fight Fereyburz, King Kavus.'
segments.s203-g63: ز (poetic از) marks source 'from'; هومان is the proper name. گریزان is the participle/adverb 'fleeing / in flight'. بشد is the past/preterite 'became / happened' here meaning 'took to (the state of) flight' — the verb applies to پهلوان 'the champion', which appears after the verb in this poetic order: 'From Homan the champion became fleeing' → 'the champion fled from Homan.'
segments.s203-g64: شکست is the noun 'defeat' (subject). اندر آمد is a compound 'entered / came into' (اندر = into/within, آمد = came). برزم is a locative/poetic reference to the battlefield/field of fight (here governed by 'اندر آمد'). گوان is an adverb 'like / as' giving a simile or emphasis ('as it were' / 'thus'). Together: 'Defeat entered into the battlefield, as it were.'
segments.s203-g9: This is reported speech introduced by the pronoun and the past-tense verb: تو (you) + گفتی (you said). The clause that follows is a nominal sentence with the copula attached to the last word: جهان (the world) is the topic, دام (trap) is predicated of it, and نر اژدها (literally “male dragon”) is the possessor; in ordinary prose an ezafe would link دام to نر and نر to اژدها (دامِ نرِ اژدها), but in poetry the ezafe is omitted for meter. اژدهاست is the copula + noun ('is a dragon').
segments.s203-g10: وگر is a conditional connector ('but if' / 'and if'); آسمان is the subject (the sky/heaven). بر + زمین gives the direction/place ('upon the earth'). گشت is the past of گشتن used here in a hypothetical/optative sense ('were to become' / 'were to fall'), and راست ('true' / 'indeed') functions as an adverbial intensifier ('indeed/truly'). The clause is therefore the protasis of a conditional ('and if the sky were truly to fall on the earth...').
segments.s203-g11: نبد is an archaic/poetic negative existential ('there was not' / 'there did not exist'). پشه را is the marked (definite/emphatic) object/topic 'the gnat' (را marks definiteness/emphasis). روزگار گذر is a noun phrase 'the passing of time / respite of fate' (روزگار = fate/time, گذر = passing), so the whole means 'there was no passing of fate/time for the gnat' → 'the gnat had no respite.'
segments.s203-g12: ز here is the poetic preposition 'because of / from', and بس intensifies it ('so much / so many'). The list that follows (گرز و تیغ و سنان و سپر) enumerates weapons: گرز (mace), تیغ (sword), سنان (spear), سپر (shield), joined by و ('and'). The whole phrase means 'because of so many maces, swords, spears and shields.'
segments.s203-g13: سوی میمنه is an adverbial locative phrase 'toward/on the right side'. The two proper names گیو گودرز appear in sequence as the people located there (poetic omission of a conjunction is common), and بود is the past copula 'were/was' placing them there in the past narrative.
segments.s203-g14: This segment lists three figures at a place: رد (here functioning as a proper name), followed by موبد ('mōbed', a Zoroastrian priest) and مهتر ('mahtar', a chief or senior officer). مرز ('border, frontier') gives the location, and بود is again the past copula 'were/was'. The coordinating و joins the items in the list.
segments.s203-g15: سوی introduces a locative/directional phrase ('toward, on the side of'). میسره corresponds to the other side (here 'the left side') and is followed by three names (میسره اشکش تیزچنگ) — poetic sequencing often omits conjunctions. The sequence names the people placed on that side; تیزچنگ is a proper name (here written as one word).
segments.s203-g16: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). دریای خون is a genitive construction 'a sea of blood' (دریای = 'sea of' + خون = 'blood' i.e. heavy bloodshed). راند is the past tense of راندن ('drove/caused to flow'), so دریای خون راند = 'he caused a sea of blood (to flow)'. هنگام جنگ is an adverbial phrase 'at the time of battle'. The whole relative clause qualifies the named fighters.
segments.s203-g225: از + place marks motion away: از آوردگه = 'from the gathering/place of assembly'. روی برگاشتند is a fused phrase meaning 'they turned their face (away)' → 'they returned/turned back'; برگاشتند is a past, 3rd-person-plural verb. Word order is preposition + place + verbal phrase.
segments.s203-g226: همی is a classical/perhaps progressive/episodic particle here (stylistic), adding immediacy; خستگان is the plural substantive 'the weary'; خوار is an adjective meaning 'despised/humbled' modifying خستگان; بگذاشتند is past 3rd-plural 'they left/abandoned' (بِ‑گذاشتند = left behind). The clause says who was left behind.
segments.s203-g227: بدانگه is a temporal/deictic adverb 'at that moment/thereupon'; کجا functions here as a relative/locative 'where'; بخت‌برگشته is a compound adjective 'fortune-turned' = 'ill-fated' or 'whose luck has turned'; بود is the past copula 'was'. The phrase describes the circumstance/place where misfortune was present.
segments.s203-g228: کشته بود is the past perfect/passive construction 'had been killed' (کشته = 'killed', بود = auxiliary 'had/was'); گستهم here is a proper name (Gostaham) serving as the patient of کشته بود; دمان باره precedes the name as a locative/phrase indicating place or occasion (literal words 'skirt/foothill' and 'occasion/instance' used poetically). Together the clause reports that Gostaham had been slain at that place/occasion.
segments.s203-g229: پیاده is an adverb/adjective 'on foot' (or 'afoot'); همی again gives a stylistic/progressive flavor; رفت is the past 'he went'; نیزه بدست is a noun + postpositional phrase 'spear in hand' (بدست = 'in the hand' / 'holding'). Phrase orders the manner of his going.
segments.s203-g230: ابا + object denotes refusal/reluctance: 'he refused the jošan (cuirass/armor)'; و is the coordinating 'and'; خود emphasizes 'himself' or 'personally'; برسان here functions as a verb meaning 'reach/bring oneself' in context (poetic usage), and مست is a predicate adjective 'drunk' modifying the subject. The clause conveys that he would not take armor and was himself drunken as he proceeded.
segments.s203-g231: چو is the conjunction 'when'; بیژن is the proper name 'Bijan' (subject); بگستهم here is the poetic concatenation of the prepositional prefix بَ- + گستهم (i.e. 'toward Gostaham'), so the phrase means 'approached Gostaham'; نزدیک شد is the verb phrase 'became near / approached' (شد = past auxiliary/verb). The whole is a temporal clause introducing what follows.
segments.s203-g232: شب آمد is a simple past clause 'night came'; همی again gives stylistic continuity; روز تاریک شد is 'the day grew dark' (تاریک = 'dark', شد = 'became'). The two clauses are coordinate: night fell and the daylight waned.
segments.s203-g193: فزاید is a present/simple poetic verb meaning 'adds' or 'increases' and here heads the clause. بر این ننگها is a prepositional phrase ('upon/to these shames') with the preposition بر + demonstrative این; ننگها is the plural direct object 'shames/disgraces'. ننگ نیز repeats ننگ as a second noun with نیز 'also', emphasizing 'another shame as well'. Word order (verb → prepositional phrase → noun phrase) is typical of poetic Persian where emphasis can precede or follow the verb.
segments.s203-g194: ازین here = از + این, an older/poetic orthography meaning 'of/from this' (i.e. 'concerning this'). افسر is used in the sense 'officer' (not 'helmet') and is the noun described by ازین. و links the two items listed; کشتن is a verbal noun 'the killing (of...)' and takes ریو as its object. The final نیز repeats 'also' to show that the killing is an additional disgrace. In short, the clause lists two added disgraces: that of the officer and the killing of Rīv.
segments.s203-g195: چنان بد که is a result clause 'so/so much ... that ...' (چنان = 'so', بد = 'bad/terrible', که = 'that'). بشنید is the simple past 'he heard' (3rd person). آواز گیو is a noun phrase 'the voice/cry of Giv' where the proper name follows the noun without an ezāfe (a common poetic style). The whole segment means 'It was so bad that Giv heard the cry/voice.'
segments.s203-g196: This is a sequence of titles/epithets naming the speaker or source of the cry: سپهبد 'spahbed' (a military rank), سرافراز 'sārāfarāz' 'lofty/honored' functioning as an epithet, followed by پیران and نیو which here behave like a proper name or epithet. The listed senses for پیران/نیو in the word-data are generic ('elders', 'ney/reed') but in context they are part of a personal name/title, so the supplied dictionary senses do not apply.
segments.s203-g197: برامد is the past 'appeared/arose'. بنوی here functions as a proper name (Benoy) and so does not match the dictionary senses given for the same form; it names the person who 'appeared'. یکی کارزار = 'a certain one of the battle/one warrior' (یکی = 'one / someone', کارزار = 'battle/field'), so the whole segment means 'a certain warrior named Benoy appeared.'
segments.s203-g198: ز لشکر 'from the army/host' (ز = 'from', لشکر = 'army'). بران is a pointing/relational adverb ('of/therefrom' — linking back to the previously mentioned host). افسر نامدار = 'a famed/renowned officer' (افسر = 'officer', نامدار = 'renowned'). The sequence places origin ('from that host') before the identification of the person who emerged.
segments.s203-g199: فراوان is an adverb 'plentifully/much' modifying the clause. ز هر سو = 'from every side' (ز = 'from', هر سو = 'every side'). سپه is the subject 'the host/army'; کشته شد is the passive construction 'were killed' (کشته = past participle 'killed', شد functioning as auxiliary 'was/were'). The clause means 'many of the army were killed on every side.'
segments.s203-g200: سربخت here is an adjectival/nominative element meaning 'head‑raising' or 'proud' (literally 'having raised the head'); گردنکشان is a plural noun/epithet 'neck‑raisers', i.e. proud/rebellious men. گشته شد is a compound (گشته = participle 'become/turned', شد = auxiliary) forming a verbal passive/result 'were made / were turned into' — so combined it denotes that the proud neck‑raisers were brought low/overthrown. Poetic word order and doubled auxiliaries are common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s203-g73: نگون گشته is a perfect/passive participial phrase meaning 'have become down/overturned' (نگون = down/overthrown; گشته = become/has been). It predicates the following coordinated nouns کوس و درفش و سنان, so the whole segment says those three items 'were overthrown/laid low.' Word order places the participle before its objects in this poetic register.
segments.s203-g74: نبود is past negation 'there was not' (نبود = 'was not'). ایچ (an emphatic negative) strengthens the negation 'not at all.' پیدا here means 'visible/seen.' رکیب stands for a person associated with a mount (here 'rider/horseman' or companion in arms) and is governed by the negative verb. از عنان is a prepositional phrase 'of/from the reins' specifying what the rider would have held; the whole clause means 'no rider at all was visible at the reins.'
segments.s203-g75: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' The noun دشمن is the subject 'the enemy,' and ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase 'from every side/direction.' بانبوه شد is a verb phrase: بانبوه 'in great numbers' + شد 'became' (past) — together 'swarmed in' or 'became numerous.' The clause thus means 'when the enemy swarmed in from every direction.'
segments.s203-g76: This is a short locative clause: فریبرز (proper name) is the subject and بر دامن کوه is the locative complement 'on/upon the mountain's hem/slope.' شد is the past verb 'became/was' used here to indicate that he 'took up position' or 'stood upon' the mountain-slope. دامن کوه literally 'skirt of the mountain' = slope/foothill.
segments.s203-g77: برفتند is a past, 3rd-person plural verb 'they departed/left.' ز ایرانیان gives the group 'from the Iranians,' and the following relative-like phrase هرک زیست ('whoever was alive') specifies which among the Iranians left. The relative idea is expressed by the postposed 'هرک زیست' modifying ایرانیان.
segments.s203-g78: بران 'for/over that' (thereon) + زندگانی 'lives' names what is to be lamented; بباید is the impersonal/modal 'one must/it is necessary,' and گریست is the verbal infinitive/subjunctival form 'to weep/weep.' The idiom بباید گریست expresses obligation or inevitability: 'one must weep for those lives.'
segments.s203-g79: همی بود is a poetic progressive/existent 'was/there was' (همی adding durative or emphatic tone). بر جای literally 'on their place' = 'in place' or 'in their posts.' گودرز و گیو are the coordinated proper-name subjects. The clause states that Goudarz and Giv stayed in position.
segments.s203-g80: ز لشکر 'from the army/host' is the source phrase. بسی is a classical adjective/adverb meaning 'many' or 'much' (here 'numerous'); نامبردار is an adjective 'renowned, noted.' نیو is an archaic/poetic word here (contextually indicating 'new ones' or a group) and functions as a noun/adjective appended to نامبردار. The whole short phrase reports that among the host there were many renowned (new) fighters — the exact shade of نیو is archaic and context-dependent.
segments.s203-g25: 'یکی' here functions adverbially ('once/at one time' → 'suddenly'); 'تیرباران' is a compound noun 'shower of arrows' serving as the event noun; 'بکردند' is the archaic 3rd‑person plural past 'they did/they caused' (the prefixed b- is common in epic diction); 'سخت' is an adverbial/postpositive intensifier 'fiercely/strongly' modifying the verb phrase.
segments.s203-g26: 'چو' introduces a simile 'like/as'; 'باد خزانی' is a noun + adjective 'autumnal wind'; 'که' is the relative conjunction 'which/that' introducing the clause; 'ریزد' is an archaic finite verb form (3rd sg) meaning 'causes to fall/lets fall' (here 'makes shed'), and the object 'درخت' ('tree') follows it — poetic word order leaves out an explicit object marker, so the relative clause reads 'which strips the tree.'
segments.s203-g27: 'تو گفتی' = 'you said' (تو + past 2sg گفتی). In the predicate 'هوا پر کرگس شدست', 'هوا' is the subject ('the air'), 'پر' is a predicative adjective 'full (of)' taking the following noun as its complement ('کرگس' = 'vultures'), and 'شدست' is an archaic compound/stative 'has become / is become' (3rd sg) used to describe the present state.
segments.s203-g28: 'زمین' is the subject; the prepositional/cause phrase 'از پی پیل پامس' reads 'from/in the wake of the elephant (پیل) — پامس = trampling/tread' (presents the cause/result); 'شدست' again is the archaic 'has become' so the whole clause means 'the ground has become trampled by the elephant's tread.' Note the genitive/causal use of 'از' and the poetic omission of auxiliary words.
segments.s203-g29: 'نبد' is the archaic negative existential verb ('there was not' / 'did not exist'); word order places the verb before the locative phrase 'بر هوا' ('on/in the air') and the nominal phrase 'مرغ را جایگاه' ('a place for the bird'), where 'را' is the definite/object marker attached to 'مرغ' to emphasize 'the bird'. The clause literally reads 'There was not upon the air a place for the bird.'
segments.s203-g30: The repeated preposition 'ز' ('from/because of') enumerates causes: 'ز تیر و ز گرز و ز گرد سپاه' = 'because of arrows and maces and the dust of the army.' 'گرد سپاه' is a genitive construction 'the dust of the host/army.' This phrase supplies the reason for the lack of place in the air.
segments.s203-g31: 'درفشیدن' functions as a verbal noun/temporal participle meaning roughly 'the flashing/gleam' (poetic usage introduces a circumstance); 'تیغ' is the noun 'sword' followed by the adjectival compound 'الماس گون' = 'diamond‑like' (noun + -گون '‑like'), so the whole phrase means 'the flashing of a diamond‑like sword.'
segments.s203-g32: Read as a resultative/clause: 'بکردار' is used adjectivally ('having the form/appearance of' — poetic/archaic use), 'آتش' = 'fire', and 'بگرد اندرون' functions as an archaic verbal phrase 'turned/become within' (3rd person stative). Together they express 'the interior became like fire.' Note the archaic prefixed b- forms and the compact, somewhat inverted word order typical of epic Persian.
segments.s203-g97: بپیچیم is the hortative/first-person-plural present-subjunctive with the verbal prefix بـ (literally “let us turn” or “we should turn”). زین جایگه is a poetic contracted prepositional phrase (ز + این جایگه) meaning “from this place,” with جایگه an archaic word for “place/position.” سوی is a preposition meaning “toward,” followed by the noun جنگ “battle/war.”
segments.s203-g98: نیاریم here functions as a negative hortative/first-person-plural (we should not / let us not …), a contracted poetic form of a negative verb phrase (context implies ‘bring’/‘cause’). بر خاک is a prepositional phrase ‘upon/on the soil,’ کشواد is a proper name (the land/soil being spoken of), and ننگ is the direct noun ‘shame’ or ‘disgrace’ that is being avoided.
segments.s203-g99: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’ placed before the source (دانا, ‘the wise one’); word order places the source first for emphasis. تو is the subject pronoun ‘you,’ and نشنیدی is the simple past (2nd person singular) ‘you did not hear’ (negative already given by the context word order). آن داستان is the direct object ‘that tale/story.’
segments.s203-g100: که introduces a relative clause (‘which/that’). برگوید is a present/habitual third-person verb ‘he/it tells’ or ‘relates.’ از governs the following noun phrase and means ‘of/concerning,’ گفته is used as a noun ‘saying / report,’ and باستان is an adjective ‘ancient,’ so the clause means ‘which relates/tells of ancient sayings.’
segments.s203-g101: که‌گر is the conditional particle (archaic spelling of که گر) meaning ‘if.’ دو برادر is the subject ‘two brothers.’ نهد is the third-person singular of نهـادن ‘to place/set’ (here narrated in the protasis: ‘set/put’), and the reduplicated پشت پشت literally means ‘back to back’ (an emphatic adverbial expression).
segments.s203-g102: تن کوه را is a genitive construction ‘the body/bulk of the mountain’ (تن = ‘body’; کوه specifies whose), with را marking the definite/emphatic object. سنگ ماند is a verblike phrase meaning ‘remains as a stone’ or ‘will be left a stone’ (ماند = remain/be left). بمشت is a compacted prepositional phrase meaning ‘in (a) fist / in the hand’ (poetic contraction of به مشت); its exact morphological segmentation is archaic/poetic.
segments.s203-g103: تو باشی uses باشی (present/subjunctive or optative) with the pronoun ‘you’—here a wish or blessing ‘may you be / you shall be.’ و links the next phrase. هفتاد is the numeral ‘seventy,’ جنگی is an adjectival form meaning ‘warlike’ or ‘fighters,’ and پسر is ‘son’ (used collectively to mean ‘sons’ or descendants).
segments.s203-g104: ز here is the preposition ‘from / by / because of.’ دوده (literally ‘smoke’ / ‘soot’) with ستوده (past participle ‘praised’) forms a descriptive phrase ‘praised by smoke’ (i.e., famed through the smoke of battle). بسی is an intensifier meaning ‘much / very’ (classical use as ‘greatly’), and نامور is the adjective ‘renowned’ — together ‘very renowned’ or ‘much famed.’
segments.s203-g41: بتیغ and بنیزه show weapons with an enclitic b- (literary contraction of با) meaning 'with/armed with' followed by the verb برآویختند (3rd person plural past). The verb برآویختند is a past, active plural meaning 'they leapt up / they raised / they brandished' and links the weapon-phrases to the action (i.e. 'they sprang up with sword and spear' / 'they brandished sword and spear').
segments.s203-g42: همی is a narrative adverb/particle (here an emphatic 'just/straightaway'); ز introduces source ('from'). آهن in poetic usage names the warriors' metal/steel (not literal ore). آتش is the noun 'fire', فرو is a directional prefix/adverb meaning 'down(ward)', and ریختند is the 3rd-person plural past of ریختن 'to pour/fall'. Together: 'straightaway from the steel fire poured down.'
segments.s203-g43: چو ... شد forms a temporal clause 'when ... happened'. رزم is the noun 'battle/fight'; the names follow in normal Persian order (name then adjective), so 'رزم گودرز و پیران درشت' is 'the battle of Goudarz and the stout Piran.' شد is the past/auxiliary meaning 'occurred' or 'took place'.
segments.s203-g44: چو here introduces an approximate/compare sense ('as many as' / 'about'), followed by the quantity phrase نهصد تن 'nine hundred persons'. از تخم پیران literally 'of the seed/offspring of Piran' (تخم used metaphorically for lineage). بکشت is an archaic past form (contextually 'he killed'); the verb form and lemma as listed are irregular/archaic in the entry, so its precise morphological analysis is older Persian (past action 'killed').
segments.s203-g45: چو دیدند is 'when they saw' (چو = 'when'); the following names لهاک و فرشیدورد are the subjects who perceive. دیدند is 3rd person plural past 'they saw'. Note لهاک in the entry appears lexically as a common noun sense but here functions as a proper name (see senses).
segments.s203-g46: کزان = 'from which / from them' (relative/adverbial 'from that source'); لشکر گشن is a nominal phrase 'the hungry host/army' (گشن = hungry). برخاست is 3rd-person past 'rose up' or 'arose', and گرد here is an adverb meaning 'around' (indicating they gathered round). The clause describes the host rising up from that place/point.
segments.s203-g47: یکی حمله بردند is 'they made one attack' or 'they launched an attack' (یکی = one/a single, حمله = attack, بردند here = carried/took — used with حمله to mean 'launched'). برسوی گیو = 'toward Giv' (برسوی = toward; گیو is the proper name Giv).
segments.s203-g48: بران is a demonstrative/adverbial 'upon/against that' referring back to the target; گرزداران is plural 'mace‑bearers' (those who wield maces). و connects the two groups; شیران here is used metaphorically for valiant fighters 'lions/brave men', and نیو is an adjectival qualifier meaning 'new/young' (regional/poetic form for 'young'). The whole segment specifies the foes at whom the attack was directed.
segments.s203-g81: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The two personal names گودرز and کشواد stand side by side as the (implicit) subjects; no finite verb appears here because Persian epic often postpones or omits the verb for poetic effect. بر قلبگاه is a prepositional phrase ('on/at the heart/center') giving the location of the action that will be completed by the following noun phrase.
segments.s203-g82: This is a noun phrase naming the object placed: درفش 'banner/standard' followed by the owner/name فریبرز; کاوس شاه is an appositive identifying him as 'Kay Kāvus the king'. Classical Persian often strings owner/epithet after the head noun without an overt ezāfe in epic style.
segments.s203-g83: ندید is the past negative 'did not see.' The conjunction و links the clauses; یلان سپه را is the definite object 'the heroes of the host' marked by را. The verb ندید is repeated after the object for emphasis/clarity (a common poetic pattern: verb–and–object–verb).
segments.s203-g84: بکردار is a circumstantial adverbial formed from ب- + کردار meaning 'with/ in (a certain) bearing/ conduct' (here: 'with valorous bearing'). آتش دلش is a possessive noun phrase 'the fire/ardor of his heart' (دل + enclitic ش = 'his'). بردمید is a past verb 'seized/took up' (here used metaphorically of rousing or seizing his inner ardor). Word order: adverbial → object → verb.
segments.s203-g85: عنان کرد is a verb phrase 'he took/handled the reins' (عنان = reins, کرد = did/took). پیچان is a participial/adverbial modifier 'twisting' describing how he acted. براه گریز is a prepositional-direction phrase 'toward the path/way of flight' (براه = toward/on the road, گریز = flight/escape).
segments.s203-g86: برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'arose/issued forth.' ز is the poetic form of 'from.' گودرزیان names the group 'the Goudarzis' and رستخیز is the noun 'uprising/resurrection' — here 'an uprising (by them) arose.' The noun رستخیز follows the agent noun in apposition, a common epic ordering.
segments.s203-g87: بدو is the dative adverb 'to him' (i.e., 'said to him'). گفت is the past 'said.' گیو‌ای is the vocative 'Giv — O!' (گیو with the exclamatory ای), and سپهدار پیر is a vocative apposition 'old/veteran commander' addressed by Giv. Word order is clitic dative → verb → speaker-vocative → addressee-title.
segments.s203-g88: بسی is an adverb meaning 'much/many' or 'many a.' دیده‌ای is the perfect/present-perfect 2nd singular 'have you seen' (past participle + enclitic pronoun). The following list گرز و گوپال و تیر are the direct objects 'mace(s), Gupal (a name), and arrow(s)'; the simple conjunction و links the items. The whole segment is an interrogative content clause introduced by the preceding speech.
segments.s203-g185: فرود is used as a verbal/nominal action 'descent/coming down' placed before the subject (a common poetic compression; the verb آمد is omitted). سیاوش is the subject (proper name). چون introduces a simile 'like/as' and links فرود to the following comparison. ریونیز is the term of the comparison (here treated as a name or epithet); the whole segment reads 'Siyâvash's descent, like Rīvūnīz.'
segments.s203-g186: بگیتی = 'in the world' (ب + گیتی, a locative/existential phrase). فزون here means 'greater/more' (comparative). زین is an archaic contraction (ز + این) meaning 'than this' — a poetic inversion: literally 'from this'; شگفتی = 'wonder/marvel'; چه چیز = 'what thing' (rhetorical question). The word order is compressed and somewhat inverted for meter: literally 'in the world greater than this wonder what-thing?'
segments.s203-g187: اگر begins a conditional clause 'if'. تاج is 'the crown' (object/thing at stake). آن is the demonstrative 'that' pointing to a specific youth. نارسیده is a postpositive adjective meaning 'not yet arrived/ripe' or 'not yet mature' and modifies جوان 'youth/young man'. The phrase forms the protasis: 'if the crown of that still-unripe youth...'.
segments.s203-g188: بدشمن 'to the enemy' or 'falling to the enemy' (here 'enemy' as the recipient). رسد is a present/future impersonal/indefinite 'reaches/falls to' used in the protasis. شرم 'shame' is the abstract noun that follows. دارد 'has' is the stative verb 'has/is laden with', and روان 'soul' is the possessor/experiencer (i.e. 'shame will be on his soul'). The clause thus states the apodosis: '...falls to the enemy, shame will be on the soul.'
segments.s203-g189: اگر introduces the conditional 'if'. من is the emphatic first-person subject 'I'. بجنبم is the first-person present/subjunctive form 'I move / I stir / I hurry' (used in the protasis). ازین is the contracted form of از + این 'from this', and رزمگاه is the locative 'battlefield'. Together: 'If I move from this battlefield...'.
segments.s203-g190: شکست 'defeat' is fronted as the main event. اندر آید is a periphrastic verb phrase 'will come/enter upon' (اندر = into/among + آید = 'comes'). بایران سپاه refers to the army of Iran (the phrase is poetically compressed; بایران stands for 'به ایران' / 'of Iran' or 'to the Iranians'). The whole apodosis reads 'defeat will come upon the army of Iran.'
segments.s203-g191: نباید که is an impersonal negative 'it must not be that / it should not happen that'. آن is 'that' (demonstrative). افسر here means 'helmet' (not 'officer') and شهریار 'king' follows as the possessor; together 'that helmet of the king'. This begins the subordinate clause expressing what must not happen.
segments.s203-g192: بترکان in the poem stands for 'to the Turks' (poetic contraction of به ترکان / بهِ ترکان) meaning 'into the Turks' possession' (the printed lemma alternatives are not the relevant sense here, so treated as a contracted prepositional phrase). رسد 'reaches/comes' (here 'fall into the hands of'). در صف کارزار is a locative phrase 'in the battle-line' (در = in, صف = battle-line/formation, کارزار = battle). The clause: 'that the king's helmet should reach the Turks in the battle-line' (i.e. be captured) — which must not happen.
segments.s203-g65: بدادند is a past tense, 3rd-person plural verb ('they gave/granted') placed before its objects in this poetic line. گردنکشان is the direct object ('the rebels/proud ones'); جای is the noun 'place/position' and خوی + ش is the possessive construction meaning 'their own' (خوی here functions as the root of 'own/kinship' with the enclitic ش = 'their'). Together the phrase means 'they granted the rebels their place/position.'
segments.s203-g66: نبودند is the negative past (literally 'were not' / 'there were not') in 3rd-person plural. گستاخ is an adjective used substantively here ('the audacious/impudent ones'). با رای خویش is a prepositional phrase ('by/with their own counsel/opinion'), where خویش again is the possessive (their own). The whole segment says there were no audacious people acting on their own counsel.
segments.s203-g67: یکایک is an adverb meaning 'one by one' and modifies the verb سپردند (3rd-person plural past 'they handed/entrusted'). بدشمن functions here as the recipient ('to the enemy') without an explicit preposition, a usage common in classical/poetic Persian. جای is the direct object 'place/post/position' — 'they handed the positions to the enemy, one by one.'
segments.s203-g68: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; گردان ایران is a genitive phrase 'the ranks/troops of Iran'. نبد is a negative past-form 'there was not / did not remain' and takes کس ('anyone/no one') as its logical subject. بپای here is an intransitive verbal/adverbial phrase meaning 'to stand/abide/hold one's ground.' Together: 'From Iran's troops no one remained to stand.'
segments.s203-g69: بماندند is the past 3rd-person plural meaning 'they remained' or 'were left' (here indicating that objects remained behind). بر جای ('on/at the place') marks location. کوس و درفش are the two objects ('war-drum and banner/standard') joined by و 'and'. The phrase means 'the drum and the banner were left in their place.'
segments.s203-g70: ز is the causal/agentive 'from/because of'; پیکارشان is 'their fighting/battle' (پیکار + possessive suffix شان). دیده‌ها is the plural subject 'the eyes'; شد is the past of 'become' ('became'); بنفش is the resulting adjective 'purple' (here figuratively meaning bruised/darkened). So: 'Because of their fighting, their eyes became purple.'
segments.s203-g71: دلیران is the plural noun 'the brave (ones)'. بدشمن here functions as a directional/relational noun 'toward the enemy' (classical poetry often omits the preposition). نمودند is past 'they showed' or 'they displayed' and is used idiomatically with پشت ('back') to mean 'turned their backs' (i.e., fled/retreated). The clause means 'the brave turned their backs on the enemy.'
