segments.s203-g72: ازان is the demonstrative preposition 'from that'; کارزار is 'battle/battlefield' and is its object. انده here is an archaic/poetic form (functioning like اندوه 'sorrow/grief') and serves as the grammatical subject in this clause; آمد is the past 'came'. بمشت is an adverbial/poetic phrase indicating 'in a mass/in a handful' (an idiomatic intensifier). Together the clause reads 'from that battlefield sorrow came upon them in a mass.' Note: انده and بمشت are poetic/archaic forms and their exact lexical entries differ from modern usage.
segments.s203-g105: بخنجر is a prepositional/instrumental phrase 'ب + خنجر' meaning 'with a dagger' placed before the object for poetic word order. دل دشمنان is a genitive phrase 'دل (heart) of دشمنان (enemies)' — دل is the direct object. بشکنیم is 1st‑person plural subjunctive/optative (volitive) of شکستن used here as a resolute 'let us / we will break (stab)'; in epic diction 'دل را شکستن' is metaphorical for striking/killing an enemy.
segments.s203-g106: وگر = 'و + گر' functions as a conditional 'and if' (classical connective). کوه باشد is a subordinate conditional clause with باشد as optative/present form 'there be / exists' (classical usage). ز بن is the poetic prepositional phrase 'from the root/base' (ز = 'from' + بن = 'root, base'). برکنیم is 1st‑person plural subjunctive/volitive of برکندن 'to uproot/tear out' and is the main verb of the conditional clause ('we will uproot'). Word order places the conditional particle and subordinate clause before the main verb, common in older poetry.
segments.s203-g107: چو is a temporal conjunction here 'when'. گودرز is the subject (proper name). بشنید is the simple past 3rd‑person 'he heard' (classical past form). گفتار گیو is the object 'the speech/words of Giv' where the possessor follows the noun in poetic diction (no ezāfe explicitly written). The clause as a whole: 'When Goudarz heard Giv's speech...'.
segments.s203-g108: بدید is past tense 'he saw' (classical third‑person past). آن is the demonstrative 'that' modifying سر 'head'. و links a second noun ترگ (a rag/shred or lock of hair depending on context) placed in coordination with 'head'. بیدار is an adjective 'awake/alert' that precedes the noun it modifies (نیو, the reed) — poetic adjective–noun order is common. نیو is 'ney', the reed‑flute; here it is likely personified ('the awake reed') or part of a vivid descriptive image. The line uses compact, slightly elliptical poetic syntax, so the precise semantic connections are compressed (hence possible ambiguity whether بیدار modifies ترگ or نیو).
segments.s203-g109: پشیمان شد is a two‑word predicate: پشیمان 'regretful' + شد 'became' (simple past auxiliary sense) = 'became regretful'. از introduces the cause/concern 'about' (here 'about his knowledge and counsel'). دانش و رای are coordinated nouns 'knowledge and opinion/counsel'. خوی + ش here form خویش 'his own' (possessive) — in the input the possessive clitic is split, but grammatically این is a single possessive 'his'.
segments.s203-g110: بیفشارد is a past/punctual verb meaning 'pressed/crushed/stamped down' (classical formation with the prefix بی‑). بر is the preposition 'on/upon'. جایگه is a noun 'place/position' (diminutive or poetic variant). پای خویش = 'his foot' (پای 'foot' + خویش 'his own'); the whole phrase بر جایگه پای خویش means 'he pressed his foot upon his place' (i.e. stamped down where he stood). Word order places the prepositional phrase after the verb as typical.
segments.s203-g111: گرازه is the subject noun 'grazah' (a sow / young boar). برون آمد is a compound past verb 'came out/forth' (برون = 'out', آمد = 'came'). The coordinating و links the next verb گستهم, which is a first‑person past form meaning 'I wandered/searched' (classical, slightly contracted form). So the clause reads 'The sow came out, and I wandered/about went.'
segments.s203-g112: ابا can be a verb or verbal noun meaning 'to be reluctant / to refuse'; here it likely expresses reluctance/refusal. برته is a concrete noun 'a tuft/lock' (poetic word for a bundle or lock of hair). The clause continues with و linking زنگه یل بهم: زنگه (zangah/zange) is a nominal form meaning 'peal, clang, or ringing/alarum', یل is 'hero/warrior' in genitive relation (the hero's ring/cry), and بهم is an adverb 'together' or 'in confusion'. Grammatically this sequence is compact: verb/noun phrase (ابا برته) + and + noun phrase (زنگه یل بهم) — 'there was reluctance about the tuft, and the hero's ringing sounded together.' Note: several words are archaic/poetic and the line is somewhat elliptical, so exact propositional relations are compressed.
segments.s203-g129: بنزد (he approached) is an old/pre-classical verb meaning 'to go to someone's presence' and here takes the person approached as its object; word order is Verb + Goal (بنزد فریبرز). The coordinating و + با او بگفت links the action by adding 'and spoke with him' (با = 'with', او = 'him', بگفت = past 'he said').
segments.s203-g130: که introduces reported speech / an indirect question ('that/asking:'); ایدر = 'here'; چه = interrogative 'what'; داری = present-tense 'you have' (from داشتن) asking possession; سپه = 'army/host'; در = locative 'in'; نهفت = participial/adjectival 'hidden, concealed' (archaic form). The literal order is 'that here what (you) have army in hidden' → 'what army do you have hidden here?'
segments.s203-g131: عنان را is marked with را as a definite/direct object ('the reins'); چو here functions temporally as 'when/as'; گردان (here a troop or a unit) and یکی 'one' form a small noun phrase 'one of the troop'; برگرای is a verbal form meaning 'turn toward / urge to turn' (a causative/imperative-like sense). Together: 'When he (took) the reins, (he) made one of the troop turn.'
segments.s203-g132: برین is بر + ین (an older/poetic form of این = 'this'), so برین کوه = 'on/onto this mountain'; سر بر فزون literally 'head upward/increase' — فزون used comparably as 'more/higher'; زین = 'saddle'; مپای is a rare/obscure form (here left as an archaic/obscure word modifying the clause). The phrase as a whole gives a spatial/exhortative image: 'to this mountain raise (your) head higher than the saddle (…).' Note the compact, poetic word order where modifiers follow the noun.
segments.s203-g133: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'; تو نیایی is a negative present subjunctive/indicative 'you do not come' (نیایی = 'you do not come' in classical usage); مرا (here split in the tokenization) is the object 'to me' (مرا = me); ده is the imperative 'give'; درفش is 'banner/standard'. The clause orders are typical: conditional clause first, then the command/condition consequence.
segments.s203-g134: سواران = 'horsemen/riders' (here genitival/attributive to درفش: 'banner of the horsemen'); و = 'and' links the next noun phrase; این = 'these/this'; تیغهای = plural 'swords/blades'; بنفش = 'purple' (color used poetically for ornamented swords). Word order is noun phrases coordinated by و.
segments.s203-g135: چو = 'when'; بیژن is the subject; سخن با فریبرز گفت is a light verb construction where سخن + گفت = 'spoke/talked' with Fereybṛz (با = 'with'). The order 'سخن ... گفت' is a common poetic pattern meaning 'held speech/conversed.'
segments.s203-g136: نکرد is the negative past 'did not do'; او = 'he'; خرد = 'reason/wisdom'; با = 'with'; دل خویش = 'his own heart' (here tokenized as دل + خوی + ش; خویش= 'his own' is split in the data); جفت functions as 'to pair/join' (used here as a predicate meaning 'join/align'). The clause literally says 'he did not make wisdom paired with his heart' → 'he did not combine reason with his heart.'
segments.s203-g89: A conditional clause: اگر (if) introduces the condition, تو is the 2nd-person subject, and ز + پیران is the poetic prepositional phrase 'from the elders.' بخواهی is the present/subjunctive 'you wish' (used here to mark the condition), and گریخت is the lexical verb 'to flee/escape' (an older/poetic form used after بخواهی to express the action desired).
segments.s203-g90: Main clause with obligation: بباید = باید 'must/ought' (archaic/poetic doubled form). بسر بر is an older compacted phrase equivalent to به سر (on the head) — here treated as a prepositional phrase; بر is 'on/upon'. م + را are the split pieces of مرا ('me' as the object marker attached), and خاک ریخت is 'to throw/pour dust' (ریخت here = the action of pouring/throwing). Word order is literary (object pronoun fragmented and postposed particles common in classical Persian).
segments.s203-g91: Negated existential clause: نماند 'did not remain/was not left' is the verb, followed by کسی which with the negative verb yields 'no one.' زنده 'alive' qualifies کسی, and اندر جهان means 'in the world.' Classical word order places the verb first and the location after the predicate.
segments.s203-g92: Nominal phrase listing those who are gone: دلیران 'the brave' and کارآزموده 'experienced/tried' modify the final noun مهان (an old word here for important men/chiefs). The conjunction و links the adjectives; the head noun comes last, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s203-g93: Fixed idiomatic statement: ز + مردن 'from dying' (i.e. 'against dying') is the topic, while مرا و ترا (here split into م + را and ت + را) are the objects 'me and you' (the object marker را appears after each prosthetic consonant). چاره نیست = 'there is no remedy.' The split pronouns are a product of classical orthography/versification.
segments.s203-g94: Comparative negation: درنگی‌تر 'more lingering/longer delay' + از مرگ 'than death' + ... نیست 'does not exist' yields the meaning 'there is no delay longer than death.' پتیاره is an obscure/possibly vocative or emphatic word here (uncommon/archaic), so its exact nuance is unclear but it sits inside the comparative clause.
segments.s203-g95: Temporal clause: چو = 'when', پیش آمد = 'came before/arrived' (پیش with آمد gives the sense 'came upon/appeared'), این روزگار درشت = 'this harsh/cruel time/fate.' Word order places the temporal clause before the result clause, as typical.
segments.s203-g96: Result/preference clause: ترا + را = 'you' (object), روی بینند = 'they see the face (of you)' and better که پشت is an elliptical comparative: 'better that (they see) the back' — i.e. 'it is better they see your face than your back.' که functions as a conjunction introducing the contrast; پشت = 'back.' The verb بینند is 3rd-person plural 'they see.'

sentences.s203-s61: Goudarz the elder thus said to Bijan, "From here, go quickly and bring an arrow."

sentences.s203-s91: There was no one like that in this battlefield — a young, proud, son of the king.
sentences.s203-s92: The scion of the sovereign, old Kay Kavus — three men were slain; the wretched wailed and the onlookers stood staring in astonishment.
sentences.s203-s117: He said to him, "Stop — sit beside me; there is no one dearer to me than you."
sentences.s203-s118: Both sat upon that leaf; when the sun suddenly became dark.
sentences.s203-s119: All fled toward that mountain's slopes; the affair was turned back.
sentences.s203-s120: The Turkish horsemen, all cheerful, freed from suffering and sorrow, became light‑hearted.
sentences.s203-s105: Of the Gudarzis eight men remained alive; the others had been cast elsewhere on that battlefield.
sentences.s203-s106: Also from Giv's stock about twenty-five — who were handsome countrymen and possessed of treasure.
sentences.s203-s107: Also from the seed of Kavus seventy men — horsemen and lions of the day of battle.
sentences.s203-s108: Excepting that crowned head of Rey, it is not fitting that anyone be counted among them.
sentences.s203-s69: Someone cried out to Bijan, "Go! — for you are rash in action and inexperienced in battle."

sentences.s203-s71: The standard is not from Bijan's door; nor, throughout the world, does a ney exist.

sentences.s203-s1: When the head of the month — the appointed time for battle — came, they turned away from their covenant and from regard for name and shame.
sentences.s203-s2: A roar rose from both hosts; they all at once went toward the battlefield.
sentences.s203-s3: From so many cries, trumpet-blasts and Indian drums, the very heavens were shaken from their place.
sentences.s203-s4: Also from the manes of the horses and from hands and reins, and from Gopala and sword and bow and spear.
sentences.s203-s25: Arrows rained from the chiefs' bows upon the renowned, mail-clad warriors.
sentences.s203-s26: So it happened that no one saw the face of the land; because of so many slain, the ground became vanished.
sentences.s203-s27: One set his back against another; nor did he abandon the post that he held.
sentences.s203-s28: Thus Homān said to Farshidvard that one must fight by leaping at the heart.
sentences.s203-s81: They asked Giv and Gostaham which horsemen had been with him.
sentences.s203-s82: That the remnant must go to Turan with the army, to snatch from them the crown and the seat.
sentences.s203-s83: From the troop of Iran the valiant chiefs set out; many spearmen advanced.
sentences.s203-s84: They killed many of them; from the road came the famed Bijan (and many horsemen came).
sentences.s203-s73: The Kayanian Akhtar cut it in two; he lifted one half away from the center.

sentences.s203-s9: The warriors, with Fereyberz, King Kavus — the standard from the rear, in the heart.
sentences.s203-s10: Fereyberz, with his army, said that our skills had been hidden within us.
sentences.s203-s11: This day, like a lion in battle we will strike — we will make the world tight for the ill‑minded.
sentences.s203-s12: From this disgrace, until eternity, upon the host — the mace and the Roman helmet will forever deride (laugh at it).
sentences.s203-s17: You said the face of the earth had turned dark; the star of the heart had become a war‑elephant.
sentences.s203-s18: From the multitude of spears, maces, and sharp swords there rose — indeed — an uprising throughout the world.

sentences.s203-s20: The famed Gudarzis were reluctant; from them arose the ill way of profit and loss.
sentences.s203-s77: Homan said thus, "That one is a star in whom the strength of Iran resides."
sentences.s203-s78: If we seize the purple standard, we will make the whole world press upon the king.

sentences.s203-s102: The spear-point seized the crown; two armies remained before him, amazed.
sentences.s203-s103: It remained so until it grew dark; indeed the eyes were dazzled by the darkness.

sentences.s203-s29: Fereyburz must, from that very heart, slip away and come from the rear of the host.

sentences.s203-s5: You said the world is the trap of a male dragon, and if the sky were indeed to fall upon the earth...
